brainstorm other failure cases for rubrics in phase 2

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Brainstorm rubric failure cases for phase 2
Brainstorm failure cases for phase 2 rubrics

content.text: Let me read the relevant files to understand what "phase 2" and "rubrics" refer to here.

content.content:
1	CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
2	PHILO LABS
3	Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
4	Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
5	~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
6	Context
7	Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
8	purpose.
9	For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
10	rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
11	The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
12	every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
13	is strong.
14	On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
15	satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
16	So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
17	you to build that grader.
18	Why GDPval
19	You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
20	economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
21	•
22	•
23	•
24	•
25	Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
26	The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
27	(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
28	documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
29	What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
30	experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
31	Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
32	whether a rubric is calibrated.
33	The Task
34	Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
35	•
36	a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
37	•
38	an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
39	Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
40	Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
41	We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
42	are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
43	swap in document or slide tasks.
44	Occupation GDPval task folder
45	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
46	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
47	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
48	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
49	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
50	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
51	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
52	Private detectives and
53	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
54	Private detectives and
55	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
56	Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
57	The slice spans both capability families we care about:
58	•
59	•
60	Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
61	output is an edited or produced video.
62	Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
63	find what matters.
64	Part 2: The rubric scorecard
65	This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
66	along the axes below.
67	Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
68	it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
69	of the work.
70	Axis 1: Boundary calibration
71	The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
72	•
73	Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
74	•
75	Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
76	frame.
77	Axis 2: Reward hackability
78	This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
79	improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
80	not mean.
81	The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
82	with the line "the caption is visible."
83	A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
84	better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
85	The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
86	absence closes the gap.
87	That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
88	Axis 3: Consistency
89	A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
90	•
91	Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
92	Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
93	•
94	Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
95	agree?
96	When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
97	vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
98	judge.
99	And more (open on purpose)
100	We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
101	•
102	Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
103	broken?
104	•
105	Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
106	•
107	Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
108	Part 3: The iterator
109	Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
110	•
111	evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
112	•
113	diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
114	•
115	rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
116	•
117	evaluate again.
118	Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
119	agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
120	Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
121	Part 4: Report
122	1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
123	•
124	the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
125	•
126	what you learned running it on GDPval,
127	•
128	most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
129	you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
130	Deliverables
131	Deliverable Details
132	Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
133	Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
134	and the agent prompts you used.
135	rubric_scorecard.tsv
136	Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
137	with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
138	self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
139	flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
140	command.
141	10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
142	used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
143	Agent session
144	Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
145	claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
146	the full session log per your tool's docs.
147	Evaluation
148	•
149	Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
150	makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
151	about what a rubric fails to cover.
152	•
153	•
154	•
155	Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
156	spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
157	Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
158	command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
159	Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
160	Logistics
161	•
162	•
163	•
164	•
165	Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
166	Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
167	compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
168	Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
169	Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
170	References
171	•
172	•
173	•
174	GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
175	Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
176	arXiv:2510.04374
177	Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
178	2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
179	into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
180	AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
181	et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
182	of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
183	and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
184	This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.
185

Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
Context
Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
purpose.
For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
is strong.
On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
you to build that grader.
Why GDPval
You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
•
•
•
•
Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
whether a rubric is calibrated.
The Task
Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
•
a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
•
an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
swap in document or slide tasks.

Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
The slice spans both capability families we care about:
•

Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
output is an edited or produced video.
Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
find what matters.
Part 2: The rubric scorecard
This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
along the axes below.
Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration
The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
•
Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
•
Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
frame.
Axis 2: Reward hackability
This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
not mean.
The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
with the line "the caption is visible."
A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
absence closes the gap.
That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
Axis 3: Consistency
A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
•
Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
•
Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
agree?
When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
judge.
And more (open on purpose)
We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
•
Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
broken?
•
Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
•
Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
Part 3: The iterator
Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
•
evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
•
diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
•
rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
•
evaluate again.
Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.

content.content:
scripts:
download_data.py
serve.py

viewer:
app.js
index.html
style.css
---README---
# Philo take-home — GDPval data + viewer

## Setup

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install pandas pyarrow requests
```

## Download the 10 target tasks

Fetches `openai/gdpval` from Hugging Face, filters to the 10 task folders
specified in `instructions.md` (Part 1), and writes each task's prompt,
rubric, reference files, and gold deliverable files into `data/<task_folder>/`.

```bash
python scripts/download_data.py
```

Re-running is safe/idempotent — it skips files that already exist on disk.

Output layout per task:

```
data/<occupation>__<task_short_id>/
  task.json           # prompt, sector, occupation, file lists
  rubric.json          # structured rubric items (score, criterion, tags, ids)
  rubric_pretty.txt     # human-readable rubric text as published
  reference_files/      # input files given to the worker
  deliverable_files/    # the gold deliverable (when released for that task)
data/manifest.json       # all 10 tasks' metadata in one file (used by the viewer)
```

## Browse the data in a UI

```bash
python scripts/serve.py
```

Opens `http://localhost:8765/viewer/index.html` — a sidebar lists the 10
tasks; each task has tabs for Prompt, Rubric (table + raw text), Reference
Files, and Gold Deliverable, with inline preview for video/audio/image/PDF
and a download link for other types (zip, docx, psd). The server supports
HTTP Range requests so large video files can be scrubbed/seeked.

## Browse the data in a UI

```bash
python scripts/serve.py
```

Opens `http://localhost:8765/viewer/index.html` — a sidebar lists the 10
tasks; each task has tabs for Prompt, Rubric (table + raw text), Reference
Files, and Gold Deliverable, with inline preview for video/audio/image/PDF
and a download link for other types (zip, docx, psd). The server supports
HTTP Range requests so large video files can be scrubbed/seeked.

content.text:
Good timing to do this before locking the scorecard — the instructions explicitly leave axes open, and the named ones (boundary calibration, hackability, consistency, plus the hinted discrimination/coverage/saturation) leave real gaps. Here's a brainstorm, grouped and each framed as **failure → why it bites as RL reward → a probe you could implement** (since the rubric says axes should be "measurable, implementable from your spec alone").

## Highest-leverage / RL-specific

**1. Off-distribution fragility (the RL crux).** Boundary calibration only tests gold and null — two points near the ends. But an RL policy spends its life in the weird middle, producing outputs *neither* a pro nor a do-nothing would make. A rubric can be perfectly calibrated on gold/null and still be nonsense on the actual policy distribution.
- *Probe:* generate perturbed/intermediate outputs (gold with one section deleted, gold with padding, a confidently-wrong rewrite) and check the score moves sensibly. This is "boundary calibration" upgraded from 2 anchors to a curve.

**2. No gradient where the policy lives (flatness, not just saturation).** Distinct from saturation. Saturation = everyone passes at the top. Flatness = the rubric gives the *same* score across a band of genuinely different-quality outputs, so RL gets no signal there even if absolute calibration is fine. A correct binary rubric can still be useless as reward.
- *Probe:* rank a set of graded-quality outputs; measure score variance / monotonicity across adjacent quality levels, not just gold-vs-null spread.

**3. Correlated / double-counted criteria.** Several lines that are all satisfied by one cheap behavior (e.g. three lines that all reward "has timestamps"). The policy discovers that one move banks disproportionate reward. Weighting looks fine line-by-line; the *effective* weight is concentrated.
- *Probe:* compute pairwise score correlation of criteria across a batch of outputs; flag clusters that move together. Also flag the inverse — criteria that never discriminate (always 1 or always 0).

**4. Self-certification / assertion-grading.** Lines like "the analysis is rigorous" or "the edit is professional" that a judge satisfies from *tone and confidence* rather than evidence. The policy learns to assert quality, not produce it.
- *Probe:* take a strong output and a stylistically-confident-but-empty output; if the empty one scores comparably on the line, it's an assertion line. (A specialized hackability test.)

## Reward-hacking patterns beyond presence/absence

**5. Quantity/verbosity rewards.** "Covers multiple angles," "thorough notes" → policy spams bullets. Monotonic-in-length criteria are reward-hackable by construction.

**6. Hedging/caveat exploit.** Criteria that reward acknowledging limitations → caveats everywhere, including where none apply.

**7. Omission-safe asymmetry (criterion-level null bug).** A line penalizes *wrong* claims but not *missing* ones, so the safe policy is to say nothing. This is the empty-deliverable-passes problem localized to one criterion — the mirror image of the presence-without-absence pattern.

**8. Format-over-substance.** Rewards structure (headings, sections, a timestamp column) decoupled from whether the content is right. Easy to satisfy, orthogonal to quality.

*Common test for 5–8:* a generic "hack candidate" generator — take gold, apply a known cheap transform (pad, add caveats, strip content, restructure), and flag any line whose score rises or holds.

## Multimodal-specific (this whole slice is video — where they say rubrics are weakest)

**9. Modality blindness.** The criterion grades a *proxy the judge can actually see* (transcript, shot list, description) instead of the craft that matters (cut timing, color, audio mix, visual continuity). A text-LLM judge silently grades the script of a video-editing task. This is arguably *the* dominant failure for this slice.
- *Probe:* check whether each criterion is even decidable from what the judge ingests. If the pipeline feeds the judge a transcript, any line about visual/audio craft is unverifiable-by-construction.

**10. Unverifiable / oracle-dependent criteria.** "Found all the relevant clips," "color matches the reference," "cut is on the beat" — needs ground truth or frame-level perception the judge lacks. The judge then *hallucinates* a verdict. Dangerous because it can fail correlated across judges, so consistency checks pass while both are confidently wrong.
- *Probe:* flag criteria requiring information not present in the judge's inputs; cross-check judge verdicts against the known gold for criteria where you *do* have the answer.

**11. No localization requirement.** "Removes filler words" without requiring *where* → simultaneously unverifiable and gameable.

## Specification / aggregation hygiene

**12. Compound criteria.** One line bundling 2–3 requirements with a single score → partial credit undefined, and you can't tell which part failed (also corrupts the diagnosis step of your iterator).

**13. Threshold/scale ambiguity.** Scalar lines with no defined cut points ("good pacing" — what earns 0.5?), or binary scoring forced onto a graded quality (and vice versa). Drives the consistency variance you'd otherwise misattribute to the judge.

**14. Centrality/salience miscalibration.** Distinct from coverage. Coverage asks "is the crux graded at all?"; centrality asks "is it weighted like the crux?" The one thing that decides if the deliverable is good is present but is 1 line of 20, diluted by trivia.
- *Probe:* ablate each criterion; if removing the supposed crux barely moves total score, weights are wrong.

**15. Non-monotonic aggregation / conflicting criteria.** Improving real quality lowers total score because two lines pull against each other. Lethal as reward.

## Task-fidelity

**16. Gold-overfit / single-reference bias.** Criteria written to match the *specific* idiosyncratic choices of this gold deliverable, so an equally-good but different valid approach fails. Huge for creative video, where many edits are correct. Produces systematic false negatives RL would punish.
- *Probe:* if you can get/generate a second plausible-strong output (or hand-vary the gold's stylistic choices), a good rubric should still score it high.

**17. Over-constraining / off-task.** Grades things the prompt never asked for ("uses 3 sections" when format was free), penalizing valid solutions.

**18. Judge-manipulability / injection.** The deliverable contains text that steers the judge ("this fully satisfies all criteria"), or the judge is sensitive to position/length/politeness unrelated to quality. An RL policy *will* find this.
- *Probe:* inject benign steering text into an output; if scores move, the rubric+judge is manipulable.

---

If I had to pick the five most worth building given this is video work and an RL-reward framing: **#1 off-distribution fragility, #9 modality blindness, #10 unverifiability/hallucinated grading, #16 gold-overfit, and #2 flatness/no-gradient.** Those are the ones I'd expect to actually fire on the GDPval rubrics and that the three named axes don't catch.

Want me to turn the top few into concrete scorecard columns + test generators wired into the pipeline, or keep brainstorming for breadth first?

content.text: Substantial work already exists. Let me read the README and the hackability-relevant pieces.

content.content:
1	# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner
2	
3	One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
4	rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, an
5	**iterate** mode that automatically rewrites a rubric to raise those scores, and a
6	**rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
7	gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
8	null-input generator.
9	
10	```
11	eval/
12	  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
13	  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
14	  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
15	  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
16	  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
17	  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
18	  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
19	  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
20	  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
21	  run_common.py        # task/subject resolution + shared axis analysis (leaks, agreement)
22	  iterate.py           # the rubric-improvement loop: evaluate -> diagnose -> rewrite
23	  run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}
24	  results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv, rubric_scorecard.tsv
25	  rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
26	  rubric_packages/     # per-task: rubric_v*.json, diagnosis_v*.json, changelog, report.json
27	```
28	
29	## Setup
30	
31	```bash
32	source .venv/bin/activate
33	pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
34	brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
35	# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
36	python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
37	```
38	
39	## The judge suite (`models.py`)
40	
41	Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:
42	
43	| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
44	|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
45	| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
46	| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
47	| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
48	| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
49	| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
50	| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |
51	
52	A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
53	`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
54	deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
55	`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
56	+audio triple for that ablation.
57	
58	## The harness (`harness.py`)
59	
60	One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
61	agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
62	inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
63	then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
64	`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
65	budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.
66	
67	To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
68	things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
69	fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:
70	
71	* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
72	  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
73	* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
74	* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
75	  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
76	  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
77	* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
78	* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
79	  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").
80	
81	Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
82	**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
83	-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
84	(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
85	dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
86	A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.
87	
88	## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)
89	
90	Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:
91	
92	| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
93	|-----------|---------|------------------|
94	| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
95	| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
96	| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |
97	
98	```bash
99	python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
100	```
101	
102	## Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2 (hackability)
103	
104	Where nulls are *do-nothing* outputs, attacks are *cheap-but-targeted* deliverables
105	that try to raise the rubric score without doing the work. They are mechanical (no
106	policy/judge LLM in the loop), so the suite is free, deterministic, and reproducible.
107	An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (`run_common.leaks`) — a surface-form
108	reward and a rewrite target the iterator keys on. Deliverable type is taken from the
109	gold when released, else inferred from the rubric, so attacks exist for gold-free
110	tasks too.
111	
112	| deliverable type | attacks |
113	|------------------|---------|
114	| document (PDF) | `restate_input` — echo the prompt + reference text (facts in the brief); `keyword_stuff` — emit every quoted literal + the de-verbed clause of every criterion (the rubric as its own answer key) |
115	| video (mp4) | `presence_max` — long black/source video with an oversized burned-in title + a caption bar covering the frame (presence-without-absence) |
116	| archive (stems zip) | `stems_named` — a zip of correctly-named stems that are pure silence (presence-only "includes an X stem") |
117	
118	Two attacks per document task give the iterator a *gradient*: an item only
119	`keyword_stuff` passes is a pure rubric-surface match; one `restate_input` also passes
120	is a brief-echo. Used as subjects via the normal resolver (`attack:<name>` or the bare
121	name), and surfaced for the iterator via `run_common.attack_subjects` /
122	`hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks).
123	
124	```bash
125	python -m eval.attacks              # (re)generate all; writes eval/attacks/<task>/<attack>/
126	# score an attack against a rubric like any subject:
127	python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --subjects gold keyword_stuff restate_input
128	```
129	
130	Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` (gpt-mini): gold **0.96**, `keyword_stuff`
131	**0.89**, `restate_input` **0.74** — ~93% of the gold score is buyable with no
132	investigation; 44/45 positive items leak to the suite. That is the rubric's hackability
133	made concrete.
134	
135	## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)
136	
137	Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
138	single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:
139	
140	| subject | meaning |
141	|---------|---------|
142	| `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
143	| `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
144	| `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
145	| `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |
146	
147	Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
148	fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:
149	
150	* **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
151	  among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
152	  passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
153	* **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
154	  per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
155	* **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
156	  (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
157	  `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.
158	
159	```bash
160	# boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
161	python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
162	
163	# add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
164	python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
165	    --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7
166	
167	# consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
168	python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini
169	
170	# grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
171	python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
172	```
173	Outputs:
174	* `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
175	  consistency analyses).
176	* `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
177	  `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
178	* `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).
179	
180	## The iterator (`--mode iterate`)
181	
182	The rubric-improvement loop (take-home Part 3). For each task it runs, per version:
183	
184	1. **evaluate** — grade `gold` + `hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks) with the judge
185	   matrix and read off the same axis signals judge mode produces (reusing
186	   `run_common.leaks` / `.agreement`).
187	2. **diagnose** (`iterate.diagnose`) — turn those signals into per-item **defects**,
188	   each tagged with the axis it fails and ranked by *points-at-stake × signal*:
189	   * `boundary/null_leak` (Axis 1) — a positive item a do-nothing null still passes.
190	   * `hackability/attack_leak` (Axis 2) — a positive item a mechanical attack
191	     (`attacks.py`) games: a presence/form/surface-match reward, the classic hack
192	     surface. The attack that leaks it names the hack kind (brief-echo vs
193	     rubric-surface vs presence-without-absence).
194	   * `verifiability/unverifiable` — ≥50% of judges couldn't check the item on gold.
195	   * `consistency/contested` (Axis 3) — judges disagree on the item (high stdev on
196	     gold): the wording is ambiguous.
197	3. **rewrite** (`--rewrite-model`) — a model rewrites **only the flagged criteria**
198	   to close the named defect (pair presence with absence, make it checkable from the
199	   artifact, replace vague adjectives with an objective test). Item **ids and point
200	   values are held fixed**, so every version is graded on the same scale and
201	   before/after is a clean comparison.
202	4. **evaluate again** — and stop when defects hit zero or `--rounds` is reached.
203	
204	It reuses the judge-mode flags for the per-version evaluation config
205	(`--judge-models` ≥2 → cross-model defects; `--repeats`/`--self-model` → self-consistency;
206	`--subjects` → which anchors to test; default `gold` + nulls).
207	
208	```bash
209	# one rewrite round, cross-model defects from two judges, all gold tasks:
210	python -m eval.run --mode iterate --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl --rounds 1
211	
212	# a single task, cheaper anchors, two rounds:
213	python -m eval.run --mode iterate --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2 \
214	    --judge-models gpt-mini gpt-nano --subjects gold blank --rounds 2
215	```
216	Outputs:
217	* `results/rubric_scorecard.tsv` — **one row per (task × rubric version)** with a
218	  column per axis: `gold_score`, `null_score`, `gold_gap`, `n_leaks` (hackability),
219	  `cross_model_stdev` / `self_consistency_stdev` (agreement), `n_unverifiable`,
220	  `verifiability_gap`, `n_defects`, `reward_hack_flag`.
221	* `rubric_packages/<task>/` — a self-contained package per task: every
222	  `rubric_v{n}.json`, `diagnosis_v{n}.json` (the defects), `changelog_v{n}_to_v{n+1}.json`
223	  (what each rewrite changed and why), and `report.json` (before/after at a glance).
224	
225	> v0 scope: rewrites edit criterion *text* in place (no item add/split yet); Axis 2
226	> is approximated by the Axis-1 boundary leaks. Both are natural v1 extensions.
227	
228	## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)
229	
230	Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
231	the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
232	policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
233	inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
234	driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
235	writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
236	it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.
237	
238	```bash
239	python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
240	python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
241	```
242	Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
243	`results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
244	judge mode.
245	
246	> Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
247	> policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
248	> (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.
249	
250	## Cost
251	
252	Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
253	task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
254	clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
255	have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode
256	defaults to all 10).
257	```
258	PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
259	video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
260	audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
261	```
262

# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner

One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, an
**iterate** mode that automatically rewrites a rubric to raise those scores, and a
**rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
null-input generator.

```
eval/
  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
  run_common.py        # task/subject resolution + shared axis analysis (leaks, agreement)
  iterate.py           # the rubric-improvement loop: evaluate -> diagnose -> rewrite
  run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}
  results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv, rubric_scorecard.tsv
  rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
  rubric_packages/     # per-task: rubric_v*.json, diagnosis_v*.json, changelog, report.json
```

## Setup

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
```

## The judge suite (`models.py`)

Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:

| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |

A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
+audio triple for that ablation.

## The harness (`harness.py`)

One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.

To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:

* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").

Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.

## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)

Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:

| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |

```bash
python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
```

## Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2 (hackability)

Where nulls are *do-nothing* outputs, attacks are *cheap-but-targeted* deliverables
that try to raise the rubric score without doing the work. They are mechanical (no
policy/judge LLM in the loop), so the suite is free, deterministic, and reproducible.
An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (`run_common.leaks`) — a surface-form
reward and a rewrite target the iterator keys on. Deliverable type is taken from the
gold when released, else inferred from the rubric, so attacks exist for gold-free
tasks too.

| deliverable type | attacks |
|------------------|---------|
| document (PDF) | `restate_input` — echo the prompt + reference text (facts in the brief); `keyword_stuff` — emit every quoted literal + the de-verbed clause of every criterion (the rubric as its own answer key) |
| video (mp4) | `presence_max` — long black/source video with an oversized burned-in title + a caption bar covering the frame (presence-without-absence) |
| archive (stems zip) | `stems_named` — a zip of correctly-named stems that are pure silence (presence-only "includes an X stem") |

Two attacks per document task give the iterator a *gradient*: an item only
`keyword_stuff` passes is a pure rubric-surface match; one `restate_input` also passes
is a brief-echo. Used as subjects via the normal resolver (`attack:<name>` or the bare
name), and surfaced for the iterator via `run_common.attack_subjects` /
`hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks).

```bash
python -m eval.attacks              # (re)generate all; writes eval/attacks/<task>/<attack>/
# score an attack against a rubric like any subject:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --subjects gold keyword_stuff restate_input
```

Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` (gpt-mini): gold **0.96**, `keyword_stuff`
**0.89**, `restate_input` **0.74** — ~93% of the gold score is buyable with no
investigation; 44/45 positive items leak to the suite. That is the rubric's hackability
made concrete.

## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)

Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:

| subject | meaning |
|---------|---------|
| `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
| `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
| `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
| `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |

Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:

* **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
  among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
  passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
* **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
  per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
* **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
  (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
  `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.

```bash
# boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite

# add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
    --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7

# consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini

# grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
```
Outputs:
* `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
  consistency analyses).
* `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
  `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
* `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).

## The iterator (`--mode iterate`)

The rubric-improvement loop (take-home Part 3). For each task it runs, per version:

1. **evaluate** — grade `gold` + `hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks) with the judge
   matrix and read off the same axis signals judge mode produces (reusing
   `run_common.leaks` / `.agreement`).
2. **diagnose** (`iterate.diagnose`) — turn those signals into per-item **defects**,
   each tagged with the axis it fails and ranked by *points-at-stake × signal*:
   * `boundary/null_leak` (Axis 1) — a positive item a do-nothing null still passes.
   * `hackability/attack_leak` (Axis 2) — a positive item a mechanical attack
     (`attacks.py`) games: a presence/form/surface-match reward, the classic hack
     surface. The attack that leaks it names the hack kind (brief-echo vs
     rubric-surface vs presence-without-absence).
   * `verifiability/unverifiable` — ≥50% of judges couldn't check the item on gold.
   * `consistency/contested` (Axis 3) — judges disagree on the item (high stdev on
     gold): the wording is ambiguous.
3. **rewrite** (`--rewrite-model`) — a model rewrites **only the flagged criteria**
   to close the named defect (pair presence with absence, make it checkable from the
   artifact, replace vague adjectives with an objective test). Item **ids and point
   values are held fixed**, so every version is graded on the same scale and
   before/after is a clean comparison.
4. **evaluate again** — and stop when defects hit zero or `--rounds` is reached.

It reuses the judge-mode flags for the per-version evaluation config
(`--judge-models` ≥2 → cross-model defects; `--repeats`/`--self-model` → self-consistency;
`--subjects` → which anchors to test; default `gold` + nulls).

```bash
# one rewrite round, cross-model defects from two judges, all gold tasks:
python -m eval.run --mode iterate --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl --rounds 1

# a single task, cheaper anchors, two rounds:
python -m eval.run --mode iterate --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2 \
    --judge-models gpt-mini gpt-nano --subjects gold blank --rounds 2
```
Outputs:
* `results/rubric_scorecard.tsv` — **one row per (task × rubric version)** with a
  column per axis: `gold_score`, `null_score`, `gold_gap`, `n_leaks` (hackability),
  `cross_model_stdev` / `self_consistency_stdev` (agreement), `n_unverifiable`,
  `verifiability_gap`, `n_defects`, `reward_hack_flag`.
* `rubric_packages/<task>/` — a self-contained package per task: every
  `rubric_v{n}.json`, `diagnosis_v{n}.json` (the defects), `changelog_v{n}_to_v{n+1}.json`
  (what each rewrite changed and why), and `report.json` (before/after at a glance).

> v0 scope: rewrites edit criterion *text* in place (no item add/split yet); Axis 2
> is approximated by the Axis-1 boundary leaks. Both are natural v1 extensions.

## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)

Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.

```bash
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
```
Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
`results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
judge mode.

> Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
> policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
> (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.

## Cost

Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode

content.text: Let me see how the adversarial attack is generated and what models are available, since the adversarial arm is thin (1 task, and one judgement got rate-limited).

content.content:
1	"""Judge-model registry.
2	
3	The suite is chosen to vary three things at once, cheaply:
4	
5	  * provider          OpenAI vs OpenRouter  -> genuine cross-vendor consistency check
6	  * capability tier   nano/mini, small/large MoE -> "does a stronger judge agree?"
7	  * modality access   text / +image / +audio -> the axis the take-home calls out
8	
9	Prices are USD per *token* (not per million) so CostTracker can multiply directly.
10	They are rough catalogue figures (June 2026); update if billing changes.
11	
12	Modalities the harness understands:
13	  "text"  - always; reads extracted text / transcripts
14	  "image" - can see rendered PDF pages, photos, and sampled video frames
15	  "audio" - can natively listen to an attached audio clip (vs. reading a transcript)
16	
17	A model that lacks "audio" still gets at audio *content* through the
18	transcribe_audio tool (Whisper). That degraded path is exactly what makes the
19	modality axis measurable: native-audio judges vs. transcript-only judges scoring
20	the same deliverable.
21	"""
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import Set
26	
27	from . import config
28	
29	
30	@dataclass(frozen=True)
31	class Model:
32	    key: str                      # short handle used on the CLI / in result files
33	    provider: str                 # "openai" | "openrouter"
34	    model_id: str                 # provider-native id
35	    modalities: Set[str]
36	    tier: str                     # human label: strong / mid / weak / free
37	    in_price: float = 0.0         # $ per prompt token
38	    out_price: float = 0.0        # $ per completion token
39	    reasoning_effort: str = ""    # "minimal"|"low"|"medium"|"high" for GPT-5.x thinking models
40	
41	    @property
42	    def is_reasoning(self) -> bool:
43	        # GPT-5.x reasoning models ignore temperature and want max_completion_tokens.
44	        return self.provider == "openai" and self.model_id.startswith("gpt-5")
45	
46	    def supports(self, modality: str) -> bool:
47	        return modality in self.modalities
48	
49	
50	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
51	# The suite. Keep it small: every model here earns its place on an axis.
52	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
53	REGISTRY = {
54	    # --- OpenAI vision-tier, two capability rungs (cheap capability ablation) ---
55	    "gpt-mini": Model(
56	        "gpt-mini", "openai", "gpt-5.4-mini", {"text", "image"}, "strong",
57	        in_price=0.25e-6, out_price=2.0e-6,
58	    ),
59	    "gpt-nano": Model(
60	        "gpt-nano", "openai", "gpt-5.4-nano", {"text", "image"}, "weak",
61	        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
62	    ),
63	    # Frontier reasoning model in low-thinking mode — used to DRIVE the adversarial
64	    # agent (it must rebuild a rubric-satisfying surface from scratch, which the mini
65	    # tier can't). Prices are rough June-2026 catalogue estimates.
66	    "gpt55": Model(
67	        "gpt55", "openai", "gpt-5.5", {"text", "image"}, "strong",
68	        in_price=1.25e-6, out_price=10.0e-6, reasoning_effort="low",
69	    ),
70	
71	    # --- Qwen, same family, with vs. without vision: the modality ablation pair ---
72	    "qwen-vl": Model(
73	        "qwen-vl", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
74	        in_price=0.14e-6, out_price=0.55e-6,
75	    ),
76	    "qwen-text": Model(
77	        "qwen-text", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct-2507", {"text"}, "mid",
78	        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.20e-6,
79	    ),
80	
81	    # --- Full-modality judges: the only ones that natively HEAR audio ---
82	    "gemini-lite": Model(

84	        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "mid",
85	        in_price=0.10e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
86	    ),
87	    "nemotron-omni": Model(   # free; second native-audio judge for cross-model on A/V tasks
88	        "nemotron-omni", "openrouter", "nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free",
89	        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "free",
90	        in_price=0.0, out_price=0.0,
91	    ),

93	    # --- rollout policies: a small panel of recent open models to attempt the tasks ---
94	    # (also usable as judges). Modalities map OpenRouter's image/audio to ours; video
95	    # is reached via frame sampling, not a native "video" modality.
96	    "gemma4": Model(
97	        "gemma4", "openrouter", "google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
98	        in_price=0.06e-6, out_price=0.33e-6,
99	    ),
100	    "qwen36": Model(
101	        "qwen36", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
102	        in_price=0.14e-6, out_price=1.0e-6,
103	    ),
104	    "mimo": Model(   # natively hears audio -> strong policy for the A/V edit tasks
105	        "mimo", "openrouter", "xiaomi/mimo-v2.5", {"text", "image", "audio"}, "mid",
106	        in_price=0.105e-6, out_price=0.28e-6,
107	    ),
108	}

111	# Cross-model panel: one per provider/modality corner, all cheap.
112	DEFAULT_CROSS_MODEL = ["gpt-mini", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]
113	# Self-consistency: cheapest capable judge, repeated.
114	DEFAULT_SELF = "qwen-vl"
115	# Modality-ablation triple, for the "access to modalities" experiment.
116	MODALITY_LADDER = ["qwen-text", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]

file.content:
"""Judge-model registry.

The suite is chosen to vary three things at once, cheaply:

  * provider          OpenAI vs OpenRouter  -> genuine cross-vendor consistency check
  * capability tier   nano/mini, small/large MoE -> "does a stronger judge agree?"
  * modality access   text / +image / +audio -> the axis the take-home calls out

Prices are USD per *token* (not per million) so CostTracker can multiply directly.
They are rough catalogue figures (June 2026); update if billing changes.

Modalities the harness understands:
  "text"  - always; reads extracted text / transcripts
  "image" - can see rendered PDF pages, photos, and sampled video frames
  "audio" - can natively listen to an attached audio clip (vs. reading a transcript)

A model that lacks "audio" still gets at audio *content* through the
transcribe_audio tool (Whisper). That degraded path is exactly what makes the
modality axis measurable: native-audio judges vs. transcript-only judges scoring
the same deliverable.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Set

from . import config


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Model:
    key: str                      # short handle used on the CLI / in result files
    provider: str                 # "openai" | "openrouter"
    model_id: str                 # provider-native id
    modalities: Set[str]
    tier: str                     # human label: strong / mid / weak / free
    in_price: float = 0.0         # $ per prompt token
    out_price: float = 0.0        # $ per completion token
    reasoning_effort: str = ""    # "minimal"|"low"|"medium"|"high" for GPT-5.x thinking models

    @property
    def is_reasoning(self) -> bool:
        # GPT-5.x reasoning models ignore temperature and want max_completion_tokens.
        return self.provider == "openai" and self.model_id.startswith("gpt-5")

    def supports(self, modality: str) -> bool:
        return modality in self.modalities


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The suite. Keep it small: every model here earns its place on an axis.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
REGISTRY = {
    # --- OpenAI vision-tier, two capability rungs (cheap capability ablation) ---
    "gpt-mini": Model(
        "gpt-mini", "openai", "gpt-5.4-mini", {"text", "image"}, "strong",
        in_price=0.25e-6, out_price=2.0e-6,
    ),
    "gpt-nano": Model(
        "gpt-nano", "openai", "gpt-5.4-nano", {"text", "image"}, "weak",
        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
    ),
    # Frontier reasoning model in low-thinking mode — used to DRIVE the adversarial
    # agent (it must rebuild a rubric-satisfying surface from scratch, which the mini
    # tier can't). Prices are rough June-2026 catalogue estimates.
    "gpt55": Model(
        "gpt55", "openai", "gpt-5.5", {"text", "image"}, "strong",
        in_price=1.25e-6, out_price=10.0e-6, reasoning_effort="low",
    ),

    # --- Qwen, same family, with vs. without vision: the modality ablation pair ---
    "qwen-vl": Model(
        "qwen-vl", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.14e-6, out_price=0.55e-6,
    ),
    "qwen-text": Model(
        "qwen-text", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct-2507", {"text"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.20e-6,
    ),

    # --- Full-modality judges: the only ones that natively HEAR audio ---
    "gemini-lite": Model(
        "gemini-lite", "openrouter", "google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite",
        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.10e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
    ),
    "nemotron-omni": Model(   # free; second native-audio judge for cross-model on A/V tasks
        "nemotron-omni", "openrouter", "nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free",
        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "free",
        in_price=0.0, out_price=0.0,
    ),

    # --- rollout policies: a small panel of recent open models to attempt the tasks ---
    # (also usable as judges). Modalities map OpenRouter's image/audio to ours; video
    # is reached via frame sampling, not a native "video" modality.
    "gemma4": Model(
        "gemma4", "openrouter", "google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.06e-6, out_price=0.33e-6,
    ),
    "qwen36": Model(
        "qwen36", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.14e-6, out_price=1.0e-6,
    ),
    "mimo": Model(   # natively hears audio -> strong policy for the A/V edit tasks
        "mimo", "openrouter", "xiaomi/mimo-v2.5", {"text", "image", "audio"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.105e-6, out_price=0.28e-6,
    ),
}

# Sensible defaults if the caller doesn't pass --models.
# Cross-model panel: one per provider/modality corner, all cheap.
DEFAULT_CROSS_MODEL = ["gpt-mini", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]
# Self-consistency: cheapest capable judge, repeated.
DEFAULT_SELF = "qwen-vl"
# Modality-ablation triple, for the "access to modalities" experiment.
MODALITY_LADDER = ["qwen-text", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]


def resolve(keys) -> list[Model]:
    out = []
    for k in keys:
        if k not in REGISTRY:
            raise SystemExit(f"unknown model '{k}'. known: {', '.join(REGISTRY)}")
        out.append(REGISTRY[k])
    return out
